issues.body: `_porcelain_changed_paths` parses NUL records correctly but emits each raw path followed by `\n`, after which command substitution, `printf`, `mapfile`, and `_registry_selftest_targets` all treat newlines as record boundaries. Git permits newline bytes in filenames. For example, a changed path named `.github/workflows/evil\n.yml` is split into `.github/workflows/evil` and `.yml`; neither matches `.github/workflows/*.yml`, so it receives no direct workflow validation and the gate may still pass because zero targets is explicitly allowed for docs/data-only changes. The same construction can hide `scripts/*.py` or `scripts/*.sh`. The new self-test only exercises spaces, not the control-character case claimed by the implementation, so it does not catch this bypass.
issues.fix_hint: Preserve unambiguous record framing through classification (for example, perform the path matching in the NUL-aware Python stage and emit typed targets, or use a reversible encoding that cannot contain newlines). Add a fixture containing an actual newline in a workflow/script filename and assert that the corresponding direct target is produced; also cover rename endpoints with such a filename.
issues.title: Newline-containing control paths still bypass registry classification

verdict.summary: The NUL-aware Git parser fixes spaces and rename endpoint handling, and its listed assertions are non-vacuous. However, it immediately converts arbitrary Git path bytes to newline-delimited text. Because newline is valid in a Git filename, this reintroduces a classification bypass in the registry self-test gate and contradicts the stated control-character safety. The change is therefore not complete or fail-closed.
